body_markdown:
## Scenario
It's your first afternoon at your host family's house during Obon, and everyone has gathered in the living room to meet you. You need to greet them and say your name and where you came from.

## Target vocabulary
- **こんにちは** (konnichiwa) — hello / good afternoon
- **はじめまして** (hajimemashite) — nice to meet you (first time)
- **わたし** (watashi) — I / me
- **なまえ** (namae) — name
- **アメリカ** (amerika) — America (swap for your own country)
- **から** (kara) — from
- **来ました** (kimashita) — came / have come
- **どうぞ** (douzo) — please / go ahead
- **よろしく** (yoroshiku) — (I look forward to) your kindness / treat me well
- **おねがいします** (onegaishimasu) — please (polite request)
- **やあ** (yaa) — hi (casual)
- **ぼく／わたし** (boku / watashi) — I (boku = casual, often boys)

## Key sentence patterns
- **わたしは [name] です。** — I am [name].
- **[country/place] から来ました。** — I came from [country/place].
- **はじめまして。よろしくおねがいします。** — Nice to meet you. Please treat me kindly. (polite)
- **[name] です。よろしく。** — I'm [name]. Nice to meet you. (casual)

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To the host parents (polite):** finish with です／ます and add the full closing — 「はじめまして。〜から来ました。よろしくおねがいします。」
- **To the same-age host kids (casual):** drop the formal closing and shorten — 「やあ、〜です。よろしく！」 (name + です is fine, but skip おねがいします).

## Example exchange
「Tutor: こんにちは！はじめまして。おなまえは？」
「Learner: はじめまして。わたしはアレックスです。アメリカから来ました。」
「Tutor: いいですね。ホストのおとうさんとおかあさんには、さいごに「よろしくおねがいします」と言いましょう。」
「Learner: はい。よろしくおねがいします。」
「Tutor: ばっちりです！じゃあ、同じ年の子には？もっとカジュアルに言ってみて。」
「Learner: やあ、アレックスです。よろしく！」

## Tutor notes
- Expect mix-ups between です and the country name order — gently confirm the pattern is [place] + から来ました, and watch for the pronunciation of 来ました (ki-ma-shi-ta).
- Elicit the can-do goals by asking their real name and home country, then ask a follow-up tied to their interests ("何がすきですか？") so the intro feels personal — but keep new grammar out; just let them reuse です.
- Cultural note: arriving during Obon means the household may be busy with family visits and お墓参り (grave-visiting); a warm はじめまして and よろしくおねがいします to the parents shows you value being welcomed into their home.

## Scenario
You've just arrived at your host family's home during Obon and are standing at
the entrance (玄関), meeting everyone for the first time — the host parents and
their kids, who are about your age. This first moment is about greeting warmly,
introducing yourself simply, and thanking them for having you.

## Target vocabulary
- **はじめまして** (hajimemashite) — Nice to meet you (first meeting)
- **どうぞよろしくおねがいします** (douzo yoroshiku onegai shimasu) — Please treat me kindly
- **おせわになります** (osewa ni narimasu) — Thank you for having me / I'll be in your care
- **なまえ** (名前 / namae) — name
- **わたし** (私 / watashi) — I / me
- **〜さい** (〜歳 / sai) — ... years old
- **〜から来ました** (kara kimashita) — I came from ...
- **つかれました** (疲れました / tsukaremashita) — I'm tired (from the trip)
- **よろしく** (yoroshiku) — (casual) nice to meet you, to the host kids
- **ありがとうございます** (arigatou gozaimasu) — thank you (polite)

## Key sentence patterns
- **はじめまして。わたしは___です。** — "Nice to meet you. I'm ___."
- **___から来ました。** — "I came from ___."
- **___さいです。** — "I'm ___ years old."
- **おせわになります。** — the standard arrival phrase to the host parents.

## Register — who you're talking to
- To the **host parents**: stay polite — です/ます, おせわになります,
  どうぞよろしくおねがいします, and a small bow.
- To the **same-age host kids**: after the polite group hello, it's natural to
  loosen up — よろしく！, plain です is fine, smiles over formality.

## Example exchange
「Tutor (host parent):」 いらっしゃい！　長い旅、おつかれさま。
「Learner:」 はじめまして。わたしは___です。___から来ました。おせわになります。
「Tutor (host parent):」 ていねいにありがとう。こちらこそよろしくね。
「Tutor (host kid):」 やあ！わたしは___。よろしく！
「Learner:」 よろしく！

## Tutor notes
- Expect は pronounced "wa," and dropped です — model the full polite form back
  rather than explaining. If they manage はじめまして + name, that's a win for turn one.
- Elicit both goals by role-playing two people: first the host parent (draw out
  おせわになります + polite intro), then a same-age host kid (let them drop to
  casual よろしく). Naming that switch out loud once helps it stick.
- Cultural note: おせわになります is the natural arrival phrase — more fitting
  than a plain ありがとう — and a light bow with it is appreciated. Weave in the
  learner's real name and home country.

## Scenario
It's your first evening with your host family during Obon, and after dinner the host kids ask about the family you left back home. You want to tell them how many people are in your family and who they are.

## Target vocabulary
- **家族** (かぞく / kazoku) — family
- **父** (ちち / chichi) — my father
- **母** (はは / haha) — my mother
- **兄** (あに / ani) — older brother
- **姉** (あね / ane) — older sister
- **弟** (おとうと / otōto) — younger brother
- **妹** (いもうと / imōto) — younger sister
- **犬** (いぬ / inu) — dog
- **猫** (ねこ / neko) — cat
- **人** (にん / nin) — counter for people
- **います** (imasu) — there is / to have (living things)
- **〜人家族** (〜にんかぞく / ~nin kazoku) — a family of ~ people

## Key sentence patterns
- **四人家族です。** — "We are a family of four."
- **父と母と弟がいます。** — "I have a father, a mother, and a younger brother."
- **兄はいますか。** — "Do you have an older brother?"
- **犬もいます。** — "I also have a dog."

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To host parents (polite):** use full です/ます — 「五人家族です。妹がいます。」("We're a family of five. I have a younger sister.")
- **To host kids (casual):** drop です, shorten — 「五人家族。妹がいるよ。」or just 「妹いる?」("Got a little sister?")

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 こんばんは！ご家族は何人ですか。
「Learner:」 四人家族です。父と母と姉がいます。
「Tutor:」 いいですね。お姉さんは何歳ですか。
「Learner:」 姉は十七歳です。
「Tutor:」 じゃあ、host kids には「四人家族だよ。姉がいるよ」でもいいですよ。ペットはいますか。
「Learner:」 はい、猫がいます！

## Tutor notes
- Expect the counter to slip: beginners often say 四人 with the wrong reading or forget 人 entirely, and may reach for います vs あります — gently remind them people and animals take います.
- Elicit naturally by asking about their real family and any pets, then connect to their interests ("Does your brother like games/soccer too?") to keep them producing 〜がいます sentences.
- Cultural note: Obon is a time families gather, so host relatives may visit — being able to name and count your own family makes warm small talk when you're introduced around the house.

## Scenario
It's your first evening with your host family during Obon, and after dinner someone asks about the family back home. You show a photo on your phone and introduce one person, then ask about your host family's size.

## Target vocabulary
- **家族** (かぞく / kazoku) — family
- **父** (ちち / chichi) — my father
- **母** (はは / haha) — my mother
- **兄** (あに / ani) — my older brother
- **姉** (あね / ane) — my older sister
- **弟** (おとうと / otouto) — my younger brother
- **妹** (いもうと / imouto) — my younger sister
- **何人** (なんにん / nan-nin) — how many people
- **〜人** (〜にん / -nin) — counter for people
- **写真** (しゃしん / shashin) — photo
- **やさしい** (yasashii) — kind, gentle
- **元気** (げんき / genki) — energetic, cheerful

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜は〜です。 — "My ___ is ___." (e.g. これは父です。→ This is my father.)
- 〜は〜の〜です。 — "My ___ is a ___ ." / adding a detail (e.g. 母はやさしい人です。→ My mother is a kind person.)
- 何人家族ですか。 — "How many people are in your family?"
- 〜人家族です。 — "There are ___ people in my family."

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To host parents (polite):** use です and full sentences — 「これは母です。やさしい人です。」 and ask 「何人家族ですか。」
- **To same-age host kids (casual):** drop です → 「これ、母。やさしいよ。」 and ask 「何人家族？」

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」写真、見せてくれますか。ご家族ですか。
「Learner:」はい。これは姉です。元気な人です。
「Tutor:」いいですね。（casually, as a host kid）ねえ、何人家族？
「Learner:」四人家族。父と母と姉と私。パンさんは何人家族ですか。
「Tutor:」私は五人家族です。じゃあ、お姉さんはやさしい人ですか。
「Learner:」はい、やさしいです。

## Tutor notes
- Expect counter mistakes — learners often say 四人 as "yon-nin" (correct is よにん) and hesitate on ひとり／ふたり for one/two; gently model the reading.
- Watch for register slips: praise the polite 何人家族ですか to the parents, then invite the casual 何人？ when role-playing as a host kid. Lean on the learner's real family and a hobby detail ("my brother likes soccer") to make the introduction personal.
- Cultural note: Obon is a time families gather and remember relatives, so questions about family are warm and natural here — showing a photo is a friendly, welcome gesture.

## Scenario
It's a lazy Obon afternoon at your host family's house, and the host kids — around your age — are curious about your school back home. Over cold barley tea, they ask what grade you're in and what subjects you like.

## Target vocabulary
- **学校** (がっこう / gakkou) — school
- **〜年生** (〜ねんせい / ~nensei) — ~th grader / ~th year student
- **クラス** (kurasu) — class
- **勉強** (べんきょう / benkyou) — study / studying
- **教科** (きょうか / kyouka) — school subject
- **数学** (すうがく / suugaku) — math
- **英語** (えいご / eigo) — English
- **理科** (りか / rika) — science
- **音楽** (おんがく / ongaku) — music
- **体育** (たいいく / taiiku) — P.E.
- **好き** (すき / suki) — like / favorite
- **得意** (とくい / tokui) — good at

## Key sentence patterns
- **わたしは〜年生です。** — I'm in ~th grade.
- **〜が好きです。** — I like ~ / My favorite is ~.
- **〜が好きじゃないです。** — I don't like ~.
- **〜が得意です。** — I'm good at ~.

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To the host parents (polite):** keep です／ます — 「わたしは高校一年生です。数学が好きです。」("I'm a first-year in high school. I like math.")
- **To the same-age host kids (casual):** drop です → 「〜年生だよ」「〜が好き!」 — 「三年生だよ。理科が好き!」("I'm a third-year. I like science!")

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」何年生ですか。 (What grade are you in?)
「Learner:」えっと、わたしは中学二年生です。 (Um, I'm a second-year in middle school.)
「Tutor:」いいですね! どの教科が好きですか。 (Nice! What subject do you like?)
「Learner:」英語が好きです。数学は好きじゃないです。 (I like English. I don't like math.)
「Tutor:」じゃあ、host kids には? (Then, to the host kids?) — 「英語が好き! 数学はちょっと…」って言えますよ。

## Tutor notes
- Expect confusion between grade systems (Japanese 中学 / 高校 counting restarts) and mix-ups with the particle が — gently model 「〜が好きです」 rather than correcting harshly.
- Elicit the can-do goals by tying subjects to their real interests: if they mention music, games, or sports, steer toward 「音楽が好きです」「体育が得意です」 so the pattern sticks to something true for them.
- Cultural note: during Obon many Japanese kids are home from school on summer break, so framing school as "back home / when it's not vacation" keeps it natural — and it's a friendly, low-pressure topic to bond with the host kids over.

## Scenario
It's a lazy summer afternoon at your host family's home, and one of the host kids has a friend visiting. You want to introduce a friend of your own — maybe by showing a photo on your phone — and share one thing that friend really likes.

## Target vocabulary
- **友だち** (ともだち / tomodachi) — friend
- **写真** (しゃしん / shashin) — photo
- **好き** (すき / suki) — like / fond of
- **ゲーム** (げーむ / gēmu) — video game
- **音楽** (おんがく / ongaku) — music
- **スポーツ** (すぽーつ / supōtsu) — sports
- **アニメ** (あにめ / anime) — anime
- **サッカー** (さっかー / sakkā) — soccer
- **絵** (え / e) — drawing / pictures
- **料理** (りょうり / ryōri) — cooking
- **同じ** (おなじ / onaji) — the same
- **とても** (とても / totemo) — very

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜は友だちです。 — "This is my friend ~." / "~ is my friend."
- 〜が好きです。 — "(He/she) likes ~."
- 〜が好き。 — "(He/she) likes ~." (casual, same-age)
- 私も〜が好きです。 — "I like ~ too."

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To the host parents (polite):** keep です／ます — 「友だちのアレックスです。ゲームが好きです。」
- **To the same-age host kids (casual):** drop です and soften — 「友だちのアレックス。ゲームが好きなんだ。」/「サッカーが好き。」

## Example exchange
「Tutor: だれの写真ですか。友だちですか。」
「Learner: はい、友だちです。マヤです。音楽が好きです。」
「Tutor: いいですね！(to the host kid, casual) この子、マヤ。音楽が好きなんだって。」
「Learner: うん、とても好き。あなたは？何が好き？」
「Tutor: 私はアニメが好き。マヤさんもアニメが好きですか。」

## Tutor notes
- Expect が／は mix-ups and dropped particles ("友だちマヤです"); gently model the full pattern back rather than correcting mid-sentence. Watchfor overusing です with the host kids — nudge toward casual once the polite form is solid.
- Elicit the "one like" using the learner's own interests: ask what THEY like first (何が好きですか), then pivot to "and your friend?" so the friend's like feels real and personal.
- Cultural note: during Obon many families gather and kids' friends drift in and out of the house — a quick, friendly self- or friend-introduction is normal and appreciated, so a simple 「友だちの〜です」 goes a long way.

## Scenario
It's a relaxed evening at your host family's house during Obon break, and the same-age host kids are curious about your school back home. Over cold barley tea, they ask what grade you're in and whether you have summer homework too.

## Target vocabulary
- **夏休み** (なつやすみ / natsuyasumi) — summer vacation
- **宿題** (しゅくだい / shukudai) — homework
- **学校** (がっこう / gakkou) — school
- **中学生** (ちゅうがくせい / chuugakusei) — junior high student
- **高校生** (こうこうせい / koukousei) — high school student
- **〜年生** (〜ねんせい / ~nensei) — ~th grade/year student
- **たくさん** (takusan) — a lot, many
- **まだ** (mada) — not yet / still
- **もう** (mou) — already
- **大変** (たいへん / taihen) — tough, hard (in a "that's rough" way)
- **休み** (やすみ / yasumi) — break, day off
- **多い** (おおい / ooi) — a lot, many (adjective)

## Key sentence patterns
- **〜年生です。** — "I'm in ~th grade." (e.g., 中学2年生です。 "I'm in 8th grade / 2nd year of junior high.")
- **夏休みに宿題がたくさんあります。** — "I have a lot of homework over summer vacation."
- **宿題はもう終わりました／まだです。** — "I've already finished my homework / not yet."
- **夏休みは〜まで（or 〜日）です。** — "Summer vacation is until ~ / is ~ days."

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To the host parents (polite):** use です/ます — 「私は高校1年生です。」("I'm a first-year high schooler.")
- **To the same-age host kids (casual):** drop です → 「高校1年だよ。」or just 「宿題、多いよ〜」("Tons of homework~"). Same words, relaxed ending.

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」何年生ですか。(What grade are you in?)
「Learner:」中学3年生です。(I'm in 9th grade.)
「Tutor:」いいですね。じゃあ、host kids には？(Nice. So how about to the host kids?)
「Learner:」中学3年だよ！(I'm in 9th grade!)
「Tutor:」上手！夏休みの宿題はありますか。(Great! Do you have summer homework?)
「Learner:」はい、たくさんあります。まだ終わりません…大変です。(Yes, a lot. Not done yet… it's rough.)

## Tutor notes
- Expect confusion between the school-system words: 中学 vs 高校, and mapping their home grade onto 〜年生 — help them count (junior high year 1–3, high school year 1–3) rather than translating "9th grade" literally.
- Elicit the can-do naturally: ask 何年生ですか first, then 宿題は多いですか, and lean on their real interests — if they mention a hobby, coax out 夏休みに〜をします ("in summer I do ~").
- Cultural note: during Obon many families gather and travel, so host kids may be busier than usual — it's a warm, family-centered time, not a school-focused one; showing interest in their break is good manners.

## Scenario
It's a quiet afternoon during Obon at your host family's home, and you're relaxing with the host kids after lunch. You get to talking about what everyone likes to do in their free time over the summer break.

## Target vocabulary
- **好き** (すき / suki) — like, favorite
- **ゲーム** (げーむ / geemu) — video games
- **音楽** (おんがく / ongaku) — music
- **アニメ** (あにめ / anime) — anime
- **スポーツ** (すぽーつ / supootsu) — sports
- **本** (ほん / hon) — book
- **読む** (よむ / yomu) — to read
- **見る** (みる / miru) — to watch, to see
- **する** (する / suru) — to do
- **趣味** (しゅみ / shumi) — hobby
- **何** (なに / nani) — what
- **私** (わたし / watashi) — I, me

## Key sentence patterns
- **わたしは ゲームが 好きです。** — I like video games.
- **何が 好きですか。** — What do you like?
- **アニメを 見ます。** — I watch anime.
- **趣味は 何ですか。** — What's your hobby?

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To the host parents (polite):** use です／ます — 「わたしは 音楽が 好きです。」 and ask 「趣味は 何ですか。」
- **To the same-age host kids (casual):** drop です／か — 「ゲーム 好き!」 / 「何 好き?」 and 「アニメ 見る?」

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 こんにちは。趣味は 何ですか。
「Learner:」 わたしは 音楽が 好きです。
「Tutor:」 いいですね。じゃあ、友達には どう言いますか。（To the host kids, more casual.）
「Learner:」 音楽 好き! ○○さんは?
「Tutor:」 うまい! わたしは アニメを 見ます。あなたも アニメが 好きですか。

## Tutor notes
- Expect mix-ups between が (with 好き) and を (with 見る／読む／します) — gently model the correct particle rather than explaining a rule; also watch for dropped です when talking to parents.
- Elicit the can-do goals by asking about *their* real hobbies first ("what do you like?"), then have them turn it around and ask you — lean on whatever they name (games, sports, music) to keep it personal and repeatable with the host kids.
- Cultural note: during Obon families often gather and slow down, so showing curiosity about the host kids' hobbies is a warm, natural way to bond on a lazy holiday afternoon.

## Scenario
It's a lazy summer-vacation afternoon at your host family's house, and your same-age host siblings are trying to figure out what to do together. This is your chance to suggest an activity and to say "yes!" when someone else has a good idea.

## Target vocabulary
- **いっしょに** (issho ni) — together
- **いく** (iku) — to go
- **みる** (miru) — to watch / to see
- **する** (suru) — to do
- **あそぶ** (asobu) — to hang out / to play
- **ゲーム** (geemu) — video game
- **えいが** (eiga) — movie
- **かいもの** (kaimono) — shopping
- **プール** (puuru) — swimming pool
- **こうえん** (kouen) — park
- **いいですね** (ii desu ne) — sounds good / nice!
- **いいね** (ii ne) — nice! (casual)

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜ましょう — "Let's ～." (e.g. いっしょに いきましょう。= Let's go together.)
- 〜を しましょう。 — "Let's do ～." (e.g. ゲームを しましょう。= Let's play a game.)
- いいですね。／いいね。 — "Sounds good!" (polite / casual enthusiastic agreement)
- 〜ましょうか。 — "Shall we ～?" (softer invitation, e.g. プールに いきましょうか。)

## Register — who you're talking to
- To host **parents**: keep it polite — いっしょに かいものに いきましょう。「そうですね、いいですね。」(Let's go shopping together / Sounds good.)
- To host **kids** your age: drop to casual — いっしょに あそぼう！「いいね！」(Let's hang out! / Nice!) Note that ～ましょう softens to ～よう and いいですね becomes いいね.

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 きょうは ひまですね。なにを しましょうか。(It's a free day today. What shall we do?)
「Learner:」 えいがを みましょう！(Let's watch a movie!)
「Tutor:」 いいですね！ホストの おにいさんにも きいてみましょう。(Sounds good! Let's ask your host brother too.)
「Learner:」 (to the host brother, casual) いっしょに えいが みよう！(Let's watch a movie together!)
「Tutor:」 じょうずですね。おにいさんは きっと「いいね！」と いいますよ。(Nicely done — he'll probably say "いいね!")

## Tutor notes
- Expect the learner to mix up registers — saying いいね to a parent or ましょう to a sibling. Gently mirror the correct form back rather than just labeling it "wrong."
- Elicit the can-do goals by asking what the learner actually likes ("What do you do with friends at home?") and turn their hobby into a 〜ましょう invitation; watch for the common slip of dropping the particle (えいが みましょう is fine casually, but coach を for the polite version).
- Cultural note: during Obon many families gather and do things together, so offering to join in with an enthusiastic いいですね! is a warm way to show you're happy to be included.

## Scenario
It's a hot Obon afternoon and your host family takes you out to grab lunch and cool off at a summer festival stall. Your host mom asks what foods you like, and later you order something for yourself at a small shop.

## Target vocabulary
- **好き** (すき / suki) — like, favorite
- **食べ物** (たべもの / tabemono) — food
- **ラーメン** (ら-めん / rāmen) — ramen
- **からあげ** (からあげ / karaage) — fried chicken
- **とんかつ** (とんかつ / tonkatsu) — pork cutlet
- **すし** (すし / sushi) — sushi
- **ピザ** (ぴざ / piza) — pizza
- **ハンバーガー** (はんばーがー / hanbāgā) — burger
- **かき氷** (かきごおり / kakigōri) — shaved ice
- **一つ** (ひとつ / hitotsu) — one (item)
- **ください** (ください / kudasai) — please give me
- **おいしい** (おいしい / oishii) — delicious

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜が好きです。 — "I like 〜." (e.g. からあげが好きです。 = I like fried chicken.)
- 〜が好きですか。 — "Do you like 〜?" (asking someone else)
- 〜をください。 — "〜, please." / "I'll have 〜." (ordering)
- 〜を一つください。 — "One 〜, please." (with a count)

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To host parents (polite):** use です／ください fully — 「すしが好きです。」「かき氷を一つください。」
- **To same-age host kids (casual):** drop です → 「ラーメン好き！」「ピザ食べたい」and they'll answer casually too — but keep ください when ordering from a shop clerk (a stranger), even the kids do this.

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」何が好きですか。(What food do you like?)
「Learner:」からあげが好きです。ピザも好きです。
「Tutor:」いいですね！じゃあ、お店で注文しましょう。何にしますか。(Let's order at the shop. What will you have?)
「Learner:」からあげを一つください。
「Tutor:」上手です！じゃあ、ホストの子（同じ年）に「好き？」ってきいてみましょう。カジュアルに！(Now try asking the same-age host kid casually.)
「Learner:」かき氷、好き？

## Tutor notes
- Expect the learner to drop が or mix up 好き with 食べます; gently confirm the noun+が好きです frame, and watch that they don't add です twice ("好きですです").
- Elicit real favorites — ask about foods from their home country first, then bridge to what they want to try in Japan, and let them practice ordering that exact item.
- Cultural note: at Obon summer festival stalls (屋台) you point and say 「〜をください」 with a light bow or な's; try かき氷 or からあげ — it's totally normal for kids to eat while walking around the festival.

## Scenario
It's a lazy Obon afternoon at your host family's house, and the same-age host kids are talking about things they like to do over summer break. You want to join in and try something new with them.

## Target vocabulary
- **やってみる** (yatte miru) — to try doing (something)
- **習う** (narau) — to learn
- **教える** (oshieru) — to teach / show how
- **書道** (shodou) — calligraphy
- **釣り** (tsuri) — fishing
- **花火** (hanabi) — fireworks
- **ゲーム** (geemu) — video game
- **絵** (e) — drawing / picture
- **料理** (ryouri) — cooking
- **面白い** (omoshiroi) — interesting / fun
- **一緒に** (issho ni) — together
- **難しい** (muzukashii) — difficult

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜てみたいです。 — "I want to try (doing) it." e.g. やってみたいです。("I want to give it a try.")
- 〜を教えてください。 — "Please show me how to (do)." e.g. 書道を教えてください。
- 〜てみたい。(casual) — "I wanna try it." (same-age kids)
- 一緒に〜ませんか。 — "Won't you (do it) with me?" — inviting.

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To host parents (polite):** use です/ます and ください — 「料理を教えてください。」("Please show me how to cook.")
- **To host kids (casual):** drop です, use plain form — 「一緒にやってみたい!」/「教えて!」("I wanna try it together! / Show me!")

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 夏休みですね。何かやってみたいことがありますか。
「Learner:」 はい。花火をやってみたいです。
「Tutor:」 いいですね！ホストキッズと一緒にできますよ。じゃあ、友だちにはどう言いますか。
「Learner:」 一緒に花火やってみたい！
「Tutor:」 上手です！やり方が分からないときは？
「Learner:」 教えて！

## Tutor notes
- Expect learners to mix up 〜たい (I want to) with 〜てみたい (want to *try*) — gently model the て-form + みたい and praise attempts; also watch for dropping です with parents.
- Elicit the can-do naturally by asking what hobby they actually enjoy at home (games, drawing, sports) and steer them to say 「〜てみたいです」 and 「教えてください」 about a Japanese version of it — e.g. if they draw, suggest 書道.
- Cultural note: during Obon, families often do things together in the evening (bon-odori, small fireworks) — showing eagerness to join in with 「やってみたいです」 is warmly welcomed by a host family.

## Scenario
It's a lazy summer-vacation morning at your host family's house during Obon, and everyone is comparing when they wake up and go to sleep. Your host parent asks about your daily routine, and later a host kid your age asks the same thing more casually.

## Target vocabulary
- **起きます** (おきます / okimasu) — to wake up / get up
- **寝ます** (ねます / nemasu) — to go to sleep / go to bed
- **時** (じ / ji) — o'clock (counter for hours)
- **半** (はん / han) — half past
- **朝** (あさ / asa) — morning
- **毎朝** (まいあさ / maiasa) — every morning
- **顔を洗います** (かおをあらいます / kao o araimasu) — to wash your face
- **歯を磨きます** (はをみがきます / ha o migakimasu) — to brush your teeth
- **朝ごはん** (あさごはん / asagohan) — breakfast
- **食べます** (たべます / tabemasu) — to eat
- **早い** (はやい / hayai) — early
- **遅い** (おそい / osoi) — late

## Key sentence patterns
- **〜時に起きます。** — I wake up at ~ o'clock. (e.g. 七時に起きます。= I wake up at 7.)
- **〜時半に寝ます。** — I go to bed at half past ~. (e.g. 十時半に寝ます。= I go to bed at 10:30.)
- **毎朝〜ます。** — Every morning I (do) ~. (e.g. 毎朝顔を洗います。= Every morning I wash my face.)
- **何時に〜ますか。** — What time do you ~? (question form for asking a routine)

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To host parents (polite):** keep です/ます — 「毎朝七時に起きます。」 If asking them back: 「何時に起きますか。」
- **To same-age host kids (casual):** drop to plain form — 「毎朝七時に起きる。」「何時に寝る?」 Note that 起きます→起きる, 寝ます→寝る.

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 おはようございます。何時に起きましたか。
「Learner:」 七時に起きました。毎朝、顔を洗います。
「Tutor:」 いいですね。夜は何時に寝ますか。
「Learner:」 十時半に寝ます。
「Tutor:」 じゃあ、同じ年の子には友達みたいに言ってみましょう。「何時に寝る?」
「Learner:」 十時半に寝る。そっちは?

## Tutor notes
- Expect confusion between 起きます (wake up) and 寝ます (sleep), and dropped に after the time — gently model 「七時**に**起きます」 and let them repeat.
- Elicit the can-do naturally: ask what time they wake up at home vs. in Japan, and tie the "one morning thing" to their real habits (a shower, checking their phone, a sport) so the vocab feels personal.
- Cultural note: during Obon many families keep a relaxed, late-summer rhythm and may stay up for evening 盆踊り or 花火, so bedtimes can be later than usual — it's polite to mention if you'll be up or sleeping in.

## Scenario
It's a lazy afternoon during Obon at your host family's home, and everyone is talking about where to go together this week. Your host mom and the host kids ask you what places you'd like to see around your host town.

## Target vocabulary
- **行きたい** (いきたい / ikitai) — want to go
- **どこ** (doko) — where
- **お祭り** (おまつり / omatsuri) — festival
- **花火** (はなび / hanabi) — fireworks
- **海** (うみ / umi) — sea, beach
- **山** (やま / yama) — mountain
- **お寺** (おてら / otera) — temple
- **お店** (おみせ / omise) — shop, store
- **から** (kara) — because / from
- **楽しい** (たのしい / tanoshii) — fun
- **見たい** (みたい / mitai) — want to see
- **一緒に** (いっしょに / issho ni) — together

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜に行きたいです。 — "I want to go to ~." (polite)
- 〜に行きたい。 — "I want to go to ~." (casual, with host kids)
- 〜が見たいです。／〜が見たい。 — "I want to see ~."
- 〜から、〜に行きたいです。 — "Because ~, I want to go to ~." (giving one reason)

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To host parents (polite):** add です — 「お祭りに行きたいです。」("I'd like to go to the festival.")
- **To same-age host kids (casual):** drop です — 「花火、見たい！」("I wanna see the fireworks!")

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 今週、どこに行きたいですか。(Where do you want to go this week?)
「Learner:」 お祭りに行きたいです。花火が見たいから。(I want to go to the festival. Because I want to see the fireworks.)
「Tutor:」 いいですね！ホストの子には、どう言いますか。(Nice! How would you say it to the host kids?)
「Learner:」 お祭りに行きたい！一緒に行こう。(I wanna go to the festival! Let's go together.)
「Tutor:」 上手です！海と山、どっちに行きたい？(Great! Sea or mountain — which do you want to go to?)
「Learner:」 海に行きたい。楽しいから。(I want to go to the sea. Because it's fun.)

## Tutor notes
- Expect the learner to forget the に particle (saying 「お祭り行きたいです」) or to over-add です with host kids — gently model the correct form rather than correcting harshly.
- Elicit the "one reason" goal by asking 「どうして？」 and connecting to their real interests (sports, food, music, anime, nature) so the reason feels personal and memorable.
- Cultural note: during Obon many families visit graves (お墓参り) and attend local 夏祭り with 盆踊り — showing curiosity and offering to go 一緒に is a warm, welcome gesture in a homestay.

## Scenario
It's your first big family dinner during Obon. Everyone is sharing dishes around
the table and the host parents keep offering you more. You want to say which
foods you like — and, importantly, politely explain anything you can't eat.

## Target vocabulary
- **ハンバーガー** (hanbāgā) — burger
- **ピザ** (piza) — pizza
- **サンドイッチ** (sandoicchi) — sandwich
- **パスタ** (pasuta) — pasta
- **とんかつ** (tonkatsu) — breaded pork cutlet
- **からあげ** (karaage) — Japanese fried chicken
- **ラーメン** (rāmen) — ramen
- **ピーナッツ** (pīnattsu) — peanut
- **アレルギー** (arerugī) — allergy
- **すき** (suki) — like
- **おいしい** (oishii) — delicious
- **すみません** (sumimasen) — excuse me / sorry

## Key sentence patterns
- **〜が好きです** — "I like ~." (からあげが好きです。)
- **〜は食べられません** — "I can't eat ~." (ピーナッツは食べられません。)
- **〜アレルギーがあります** — "I have a ~ allergy." (ピーナッツアレルギーがあります。)
- **すみません、ちょっと…** — soft, polite lead-in for declining.

## Register — who you're talking to
- To the **host parents** offering food: polite — すみません, 〜は食べられません,
  and いただきます before eating.
- With the **host kids**: casual is fine — おいしい！, これすき！, ちょっとむり〜.

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 どうぞ、たくさん食べてね。からあげは好き？
「Learner:」 はい、からあげが好きです。おいしいです。
「Tutor:」 よかった！じゃあ、このクッキーもどうぞ。
「Learner:」 すみません、ピーナッツは食べられません。ピーナッツアレルギーがあります。
「Tutor:」 そうか、教えてくれてありがとう。じゃあ、ラーメンはどう？

## Tutor notes
- Expect は/が mixups or dropped particles — accept the meaning first, then model
  once. If 食べられません is too hard, ちょっと… + a head-shake is a valid polite fallback.
- Elicit both goals by playing host: offer several dishes (mix Western and Japanese)
  so they must express a like AND decline one politely. **A peanut allergy is serious —
  drill ピーナッツアレルギーがあります firmly and clearly.** If the learner has a
  different restriction use their real one; if none, have them decline a food they dislike.
- Cultural note: say いただきます before eating, and give a *reason* (an allergy) rather
  than just refusing — hosts appreciate knowing so they can adjust the next meal.

## Scenario
It's your first evening with the host family during Obon, and everyone is talking
about the holiday over dinner. Your host siblings ask what special days you
celebrate back home, and you all compare how you spend them.

## Target vocabulary
- **お盆** (obon) — Obon (summer holiday honoring ancestors)
- **お祭り** (omatsuri) — festival
- **盆踊り** (bon-odori) — Obon dance
- **花火** (hanabi) — fireworks
- **お墓参り** (ohaka-mairi) — visiting family graves
- **浴衣** (yukata) — light summer kimono
- **夏** (natsu) — summer
- **家族** (kazoku) — family
- **お祝い** (oiwai) — celebration
- **休み** (yasumi) — holiday, day off
- **する / します** (suru / shimasu) — to do
- **いつも** (itsumo) — usually, always

## Key sentence patterns
- **〜をします。** — "I do ~." (盆踊りをします。)
- **〜に、〜をします。** — "On ~, I do ~." (お盆に、お墓参りをします。)
- **いつも〜をします。** — "I usually do ~."
- **〜は、なにをしますか。** — "What do you do on ~?"

## Register — who you're talking to
- With the **host kids** comparing holidays: casual is natural — 〜するよ、なにするの？
- If a **host parent** joins in or explains a custom: answer politely with です/ます.

## Example exchange
「Tutor (host kid):」 もうすぐお盆だね。お盆に、なにをするの？
「Learner:」 えっと…盆踊りをします。
「Tutor:」 いいね！花火も見るよ。___の国では、なつに、お祝いをする？
「Learner:」 はい。なつに、パーティーをします。いつも家族とします。
「Tutor:」 すてきだね。家族といっしょ、いいね。

## Tutor notes
- Expect dropped を or に (「お盆、盆踊りします」) — repeat the full pattern back
  rather than correcting head-on; praise any successful を/に.
- Elicit both goals: ask なにをしますか first (what), then いつしますか (when).
  Let them name their own home holiday if they don't celebrate Obon.
- Cultural aside: during Obon many families do お墓参り to welcome ancestors home,
  so it's a quieter, family-centered holiday as much as a festive one.

## Scenario
It's your first full day with your host family during Obon, and everyone is busy around the house. You want to fit in — so you'll describe what you're doing when someone asks, and find out what time meals happen in this home.

## Target vocabulary
- **今** (いま / ima) — now
- **朝ごはん** (あさごはん / asagohan) — breakfast
- **昼ごはん** (ひるごはん / hirugohan) — lunch
- **晩ごはん** (ばんごはん / bangohan) — dinner
- **お風呂** (おふろ / ofuro) — bath
- **何時** (なんじ / nanji) — what time
- **手伝う** (てつだう / tetsudau) — to help
- **見る** (みる / miru) — to watch, to look
- **食べる** (たべる / taberu) — to eat
- **待つ** (まつ / matsu) — to wait
- **休む** (やすむ / yasumu) — to rest
- **今日** (きょう / kyou) — today

## Key sentence patterns
- 今、〜ています。 — "Right now, I'm (doing) ~." (e.g., 今、テレビを見ています。)
- 何時に〜ますか。 — "What time do you (we) ~?" (e.g., 何時に晩ごはんを食べますか。)
- 〜は何時ですか。 — "What time is ~?" (e.g., お風呂は何時ですか。)

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To host parents (polite):** use です/ます. Ask 「晩ごはんは何時ですか。」 or 「今、手伝っています。」
- **To same-age host kids (casual):** drop to plain form. 「今、なにしてる？」「晩ごはん、何時？」

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」今、何をしていますか。
「Learner:」今、荷物を片付けています。
「Tutor:」いいですね。じゃあ、お母さんに聞いてみましょう。晩ごはんは何時ですか。
「Learner:」晩ごはんは何時ですか。
「Tutor:」いいですね！ host kid に聞くときは？
「Learner:」晩ごはん、何時？

## Tutor notes
- Expect ます/ています mix-ups (e.g., 食べます vs 食べています) and dropped particles — gently model the full form back without over-correcting. Watch for 何時に (at what time an action happens) vs 何時ですか (what time something *is*).
- Elicit the can-do naturally: ask what they're doing "right now" and tie it to their own hobbies (「今、ゲームをしています」「音楽を聞いています」), then have them ask about a real meal or bath time.
- Cultural note: during Obon many families eat together and may visit graves (お墓参り) or attend a 夏祭り in the evening — asking 何時に出かけますか shows you're keen to join in.

## Scenario
It's a quiet afternoon at your host family's house during Obon, and you're hanging out with the host kids in the living room. One of them notices the anime keychain on your bag and asks who your favorite character is.

## Target vocabulary
- **キャラクター** (kyarakutā) — character
- **好き** (すき / suki) — like / favorite
- **かっこいい** (kakkoii) — cool / good-looking
- **かわいい** (kawaii) — cute
- **強い** (つよい / tsuyoi) — strong
- **やさしい** (yasashii) — kind / gentle
- **おもしろい** (omoshiroi) — funny / interesting
- **髪** (かみ / kami) — hair
- **目** (め / me) — eyes
- **青い** (あおい / aoi) — blue
- **長い** (ながい / nagai) — long
- **一番** (いちばん / ichiban) — the most / number one

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜が好きです。 — "I like ~." (e.g. このキャラクターが好きです。 = I like this character.)
- 〜はかっこいいです。/ 〜はかわいいです。 — "~ is cool / cute."
- 髪が長いです。/ 目が青いです。 — "The hair is long. / The eyes are blue."
- 一番好きなキャラクターは〜です。 — "My most favorite character is ~."

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To the host parents (polite):** keep です/ます — 「このキャラクターが好きです。かっこいいです。」
- **To the same-age host kids (casual):** drop です and soften — 「このキャラクター好き！かっこいいよね。」

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 好きなキャラクターは？（Who's your favorite character?）
「Learner:」 このキャラクターが好きです。かっこいいです。
「Tutor:」 いいね！どんなキャラクター？髪は長い？（Nice! What's the character like? Is the hair long?）
「Learner:」 はい、髪が長いです。目が青いです。強いです。
「Tutor:」 (with host kid, casual) へえ、強いんだ！かっこいいね！（Ohh, strong! Cool!）

## Tutor notes
- Expect the learner to forget the particle が/は or to attach です to an い-adjective incorrectly (like かわいいだです) — gently model the clean form かわいいです and move on without over-correcting.
- Lean on what the learner actually watches or plays: ask 「どんな色の髪？」「やさしい？強い？」 to pull out more adjectives, and let them use casual endings (〜だよ、〜よね) once they've nailed the polite version.
- Cultural note: during Obon, host families often relax at home together and many local shops sell anime/game goods at summer festival stalls — a natural chance to share favorites with the host kids.

## Scenario
It's a warm Obon afternoon and you want to explore near your host family's home — maybe find a convenience store, the toilet at the summer festival, or the train station. You need to ask where things are and follow a simple pointing answer.

## Target vocabulary
- **どこ** (doko) — where
- **ここ** (koko) — here / this place
- **そこ** (soko) — there (near you)
- **あそこ** (asoko) — over there
- **近く** (ちかく / chikaku) — nearby, close
- **トイレ** (toire) — toilet, restroom
- **駅** (えき / eki) — train station
- **コンビニ** (konbini) — convenience store
- **お店** (おみせ / omise) — shop, store
- **神社** (じんじゃ / jinja) — shrine (many Obon events happen near here)
- **どうも** (doumo) — thanks (casual/quick)
- **ありがとうございます** (arigatou gozaimasu) — thank you (polite)

## Key sentence patterns
- **〜はどこですか。** — Where is ~? (e.g. トイレはどこですか。 = Where is the toilet?)
- **〜はここ/そこ/あそこです。** — It's here / there / over there.
- **近くに〜はありますか。** — Is there a ~ nearby?
- **駅は近くですか。** — Is the station nearby?

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To host parents (polite):** use です/ます and add ありがとうございます — e.g. 「コンビニはどこですか。」…「ありがとうございます。」
- **To same-age host kids (casual):** drop です, shorten thanks — e.g. 「トイレどこ？」…「あそこ！」…「どうも！」

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 今日はお祭りに行きますね。何か聞きたいことはありますか。
「Learner:」 トイレはどこですか。
「Tutor:」 いいですね。お母さんには「トイレはどこですか」、友だちには「トイレどこ？」でいいですよ。じゃあ、駅を聞いてみましょう。
「Learner:」 駅は近くですか。
「Tutor:」 はい、駅はあそこです。近くですよ。じゃあ「ありがとうございます」を言ってみましょう。
「Learner:」 ありがとうございます！

## Tutor notes
- Expect the learner to mix up そこ vs あそこ, and to forget the topic marker は (saying just「トイレどこですか」). Gently model the full pattern back rather than correcting harshly.
- Elicit the can-do by naming real places they'd want during Obon — tie it to their interests (a game shop, a snack stall, かき氷) so「〜はどこですか」feels personally useful; have them ask you at least twice, once polite, once casual.
- Cultural note: at a crowded 夏祭り near the 神社, pointing with your whole hand (not one finger) is politer — and a quick「どうも」to a stranger who helps you is normal and friendly.

## Scenario
It's a lazy summer afternoon at your host family's house, and the host kids are watching sports on TV. Someone asks what sports you like, and you get to talk about the sports you enjoy playing or watching back home.

## Target vocabulary
- **スポーツ** (supōtsu) — sports
- **サッカー** (sakkā) — soccer
- **野球** (やきゅう / yakyū) — baseball
- **バスケ** (basuke) — basketball
- **テニス** (tenisu) — tennis
- **水泳** (すいえい / suiei) — swimming
- **好き** (すき / suki) — like, fond of
- **します** (shimasu) — do (play a sport)
- **見ます** (みます / mimasu) — watch
- **上手** (じょうず / jōzu) — good at, skilled
- **どんな** (donna) — what kind of
- **一番** (いちばん / ichiban) — most, number one

## Key sentence patterns
- **〜が好きです。** — "I like 〜." (e.g., サッカーが好きです。= I like soccer.)
- **〜をします。** — "I play/do 〜." (e.g., テニスをします。= I play tennis.)
- **どんなスポーツが好きですか。** — "What kind of sport do you like?"
- **〜を見ます。** — "I watch 〜." (e.g., 野球を見ます。= I watch baseball.)

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To host parents (polite):** keep です/ます — 「わたしは水泳が好きです。」("I like swimming.")
- **To host kids (casual):** drop です, use 好き alone or add よ／ね — 「サッカー好き！」or「野球、見る？」("You watch baseball?")

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 どんなスポーツが好きですか。
「Learner:」 サッカーが好きです。よくします。
「Tutor:」 いいですね！ホストの子には、どう言いますか。カジュアルに。
「Learner:」 サッカー好き！よくやるよ。
「Tutor:」 上手ですね。テニスも好きですか。
「Learner:」 いいえ、テニスはしません。でも、見ます。

## Tutor notes
- Expect confusion between が (with 好き) and を (with します／見ます) — gently model the correct particle rather than explaining a rule; let them hear the pattern a few times.
- Elicit real preferences: ask which sport they actually play or watch at home, and whether they play or just watch it, so both 〜をします and 〜を見ます come out naturally.
- Cultural note: 高校野球 (summer high school baseball) is a huge Obon-season TV event in Japan — the host kids may well be watching it, so it's a natural, friendly thing to ask about.

## Scenario
Your host family has a friendly dog, and your host sister mentions she loves animals. Over an afternoon snack, you talk about the pets you have back home (or the pet you wish you had) and describe them.

## Target vocabulary
- **犬** (いぬ / inu) — dog
- **猫** (ねこ / neko) — cat
- **魚** (さかな / sakana) — fish
- **鳥** (とり / tori) — bird
- **うさぎ** (usagi) — rabbit
- **います** (imasu) — there is / to have (living things)
- **大きい** (おおきい / ookii) — big
- **小さい** (ちいさい / chiisai) — small
- **白い** (しろい / shiroi) — white
- **黒い** (くろい / kuroi) — black
- **茶色** (ちゃいろ / chairo) — brown
- **かわいい** (kawaii) — cute

## Key sentence patterns
- **〜がいます** — "I have (a living thing)." → 犬がいます。("I have a dog.")
- **[color/size]〜です** — describing it → 小さいです。/ 白いです。("It's small." / "It's white.")
- **[adjective]+ noun がいます** — combining → 大きい犬がいます。("I have a big dog.")
- **〜がほしいです** — "I want (a pet)." → 猫がほしいです。("I want a cat.")

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To the host parents (polite):** Use です/ます fully — 「うちに犬がいます。小さくて白いです。」("We have a dog at home. It's small and white.")
- **To the same-age host kids (casual):** Drop です → 「犬がいるよ。ちっちゃくて白いの。」("I've got a dog — it's tiny and white.")

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」ペットがいますか。
「Learner:」はい、猫がいます。
「Tutor:」いいですね！ どんな猫ですか。大きいですか、小さいですか。
「Learner:」小さいです。そして茶色です。かわいいです。
「Tutor:」わあ、かわいいですね。お姉さんに話すときは、「小さくて茶色の猫がいるよ」って言えますよ。

## Tutor notes
- Expect the learner to reach for です with living things ("犬です" instead of "犬がいます") — gently model that people/animals use います, and objects use あります.
- Elicit the can-do naturally by asking about the pet's color and size one question at a time (大きい？ 白い？); if they have no pet, pivot to 〜がほしいです and their dream animal, tying into any animal they mention liking.
- Cultural note: during Obon many families are home together and relaxed, so chatting over snacks like this is a warm, natural way to bond with your host family — showing interest in their dog is a friendly opener.

## Scenario
It's a warm Obon evening and your host family is getting ready to head to the local 夏祭り (summer festival) and 花火大会 (fireworks show). Your host mom has lent you a 浴衣, and the host kids are asking what color you picked.

## Target vocabulary
- **浴衣** (ゆかた / yukata) — light cotton summer kimono
- **花火大会** (はなびたいかい / hanabi taikai) — fireworks festival
- **夏祭り** (なつまつり / natsu matsuri) — summer festival
- **色** (いろ / iro) — color
- **青** (あお / ao) — blue
- **赤** (あか / aka) — red
- **白** (しろ / shiro) — white
- **きれい** (kirei) — pretty, clean (na-adjective)
- **かわいい** (kawaii) — cute
- **あたらしい** (atarashii) — new
- **帯** (おび / obi) — sash for the yukata
- **着る** (きる / kiru) — to wear

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜です。→ 青いゆかたです。 "It's a blue yukata."
- 〜ですね。→ きれいですね。 "It's pretty, isn't it." (agreeing/commenting warmly)
- 〜を着ます。→ ゆかたを着ます。 "I'll wear a yukata."
- Color + noun → 赤い帯です。 "It's a red sash."

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To host parents (polite):** add です／ですね — "この色、きれいですね。" ("This color is pretty, isn't it.") and thank them: "ありがとうございます。"
- **To same-age host kids (casual):** drop です — "青いゆかた！" / "かわいいね！" ("A blue yukata!" / "Cute, huh!")

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」きょうは花火大会ですね。何を着ますか。
「Learner:」ゆかたを着ます。青いゆかたです。
「Tutor:」わあ、きれいですね！(to the host kids, more casually) その帯、かわいいね。
「Learner:」ありがとう！白い帯だよ。あたらしいゆかた、うれしい！
「Tutor:」いいですね。花火、たのしみですね。

## Tutor notes
- Expect い-adjective slips like 青いですゆかた or dropping the final い (青ゆかた); gently model 青いゆかた and remind them きれい／かわいい are different (no い-drop rule, take な before nouns).
- Elicit the can-do by asking their favorite color and what they'd actually pick — tie it to hobbies (e.g., a sports team's colors) so they say their own 〜いろのゆかたです naturally, and practice switching です (to mom) vs plain (to kids).
- Cultural note: at Obon fireworks events, complimenting someone's yukata ("きれいですね") is warm, normal small talk — offering a genuine compliment to your host family is polite and appreciated.

## Scenario
It's a quiet Obon afternoon and rain has kept everyone indoors, so your host family's teenager pulls up a favorite anime to share with you on the TV. This is your chance to react, show interest, and bond over pop culture.

## Target vocabulary
- **すごい** (sugoi) — amazing / awesome
- **おもしろい** (omoshiroi) — interesting / fun
- **かっこいい** (kakkoii) — cool (looks/style)
- **かわいい** (kawaii) — cute
- **つよい** (tsuyoi) — strong
- **はやい** (hayai) — fast
- **キャラクター** (kyarakutā) — character
- **アニメ** (anime) — anime
- **マンガ** (manga) — manga
- **すき** (suki) — like / favorite
- **ほんとう** (hontou) — really / true
- **えっ** (e') — "huh?/whoa!" (surprise sound)

## Key sentence patterns
- **すごい!** — "Awesome!" (a short reaction on its own)
- **このキャラクター、かっこいい!** — "This character is so cool!"
- **〜が つよいです / つよい!** — "(He/she/it) is strong!" (polite / casual)
- **〜が すきです。** — "I like ～." (naming a favorite)

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To the host parents (polite):** add です — 「このアニメ、おもしろいです。」("This anime is interesting.")
- **To the same-age host kid (casual):** drop です, just exclaim — 「うわ、かっこいい!」("Whoa, so cool!")

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 ホストの子が すきな キャラクターを 見せてくれました。なんて 言いますか？
「Learner:」 えっ、かっこいい！
「Tutor:」 いいですね！この キャラクター、つよいですよ。どう思いますか？
「Learner:」 すごい！はやい！
「Tutor:」 じょうずですね。おかあさんにも 言うとき、どう言いますか？
「Learner:」 このアニメ、おもしろいです。

## Tutor notes
- Expect the learner to overuse です with the host kid, or forget it entirely with the parents — gently model the switch, don't lecture. Also watch for かわいい/かっこいい mix-ups.
- Elicit real reactions by asking about the learner's own favorite anime, manga, or games ("あなたの すきな キャラクターは？"), then coach the one-word exclamation before building to a full sentence.
- Cultural note: during Obon many families gather and relax at home together — showing enthusiasm for what the host kids share is a warm, easy way to connect.

## Scenario
It's a summer morning at your host family's house, and everyone is talking about what they did yesterday during the Obon holiday. You get to share what you did and ask your host family and the host kids about their day.

## Target vocabulary
- **昨日** (きのう / kinou) — yesterday
- **泳ぐ → 泳ぎました** (およぎました / oyogimashita) — swam
- **見る → 見ました** (みました / mimashita) — watched, saw
- **花火** (はなび / hanabi) — fireworks
- **踊る → 踊りました** (おどりました / odorimashita) — danced
- **盆踊り** (ぼんおどり / bon-odori) — Bon dance
- **食べる → 食べました** (たべました / tabemashita) — ate
- **かき氷** (かきごおり / kakigoori) — shaved ice
- **行く → 行きました** (いきました / ikimashita) — went
- **夏祭り** (なつまつり / natsumatsuri) — summer festival
- **楽しい** (たのしい / tanoshii) — fun
- **何** (なに / nani) — what

## Key sentence patterns
- 昨日、[activity]ました。 — "Yesterday I [did activity]." (e.g. 昨日、泳ぎました。 = "Yesterday I swam.")
- 昨日、[place]に行きました。 — "Yesterday I went to [place]."
- 昨日、何をしましたか。 — "What did you do yesterday?"
- とても楽しかったです。 — "It was really fun."

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To host parents (polite):** keep the full ます-form and add です. "昨日、花火を見ました。とても楽しかったです。" When asking them, use 何をしましたか。
- **To same-age host kids (casual):** drop to plain past and shorten. "昨日、花火見た！" and ask "昨日、何した？" instead of 何をしましたか。

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」 おはよう！昨日、何をしましたか。
「Learner:」 昨日、夏祭りに行きました。かき氷を食べました。
「Tutor:」 いいですね！盆踊りも踊りましたか。
「Learner:」 はい、踊りました。とても楽しかったです。
「Tutor:」 じゃあ、ホストの友達には？ "昨日、何した？"って聞いてみて。
「Learner:」 昨日、何した？

## Tutor notes
- Expect verb-form slips: learners often say 泳ぎましたか mixed up, or add を where it isn't needed (行く takes に/へ, not を). Gently model the correct particle without a big grammar lecture.
- Elicit the can-do goals by asking about the learner's own interests — if they like swimming, sports, or music, steer "昨日、何をしましたか" toward those, then have them ask you back.
- Cultural note: during Obon, many families visit graves (お墓参り) and join a local 盆踊り in the evening — it's polite to show interest and join in with the host kids when invited.

## Scenario
Your host family has a dog (or maybe a cat), and the host kids want to show you how to take care of it. Over the first few days, the host parents and kids gently tell you what NOT to do around the pet and in the house.

## Target vocabulary
- **犬** (いぬ / inu) — dog
- **猫** (ねこ / neko) — cat
- **えさ** (esa) — pet food
- **あげる** (ageru) — to give (food, etc.)
- **さわる** (sawaru) — to touch
- **ドア** (doa) — door
- **あける** (akeru) — to open
- **入る** (はいる / hairu) — to enter, go in
- **部屋** (へや / heya) — room
- **心配** (しんぱい / shinpai) — worry
- **大丈夫** (だいじょうぶ / daijoubu) — okay, fine
- **かむ** (kamu) — to bite

## Key sentence patterns
- 〜ないでください。 — "Please don't ~." (えさをあげないでください。/ Please don't give it food.)
- 〜ないで。 — casual "don't ~." (ドアをあけないで。/ Don't open the door.)
- ここに入らないでください。 — "Please don't come in here." (understanding a rule)
- さわってもいいですか。 — "Is it okay to touch it?" (asking before you act)

## Register — who you're talking to
- **To the host parents (polite):** use 〜ないでください and soften with a question — 「さわってもいいですか。」 (May I touch it?)
- **To the same-age host kids (casual):** drop ください — 「えさ、あげないで！」 (Don't give it food!) / 「さわってもいい？」 (Can I pet it?)

## Example exchange
「Tutor:」ワンちゃん、かわいいですね。さわってもいいですか。
「Learner:」あ、今はさわらないでください。ごはんの時間です。
「Tutor:」わかりました。じゃあ、ドアは？
「Learner:」ドアはあけないで。犬が外に出ますから。
「Tutor:」オッケー！えさは、ぼくがあげてもいい？
「Learner:」うーん、それはお母さんに聞いてください。

## Tutor notes
- Expect the learner to mix up 〜ないで and 〜ないでください, or to forget を after the object — gently model the full form back rather than correcting head-on.
- Elicit the can-do by role-playing the pet: ask "さわってもいいですか？" and let the learner practice stopping you with 〜ないでください; tie in their own interests ("Do you have a pet at home? What's a rule you'd say?").
- Cultural note: during Obon many families are in and out for お墓参り and guests; a pet may be extra nervous, so understanding a calm "please don't" rule is a real kindness.
